Forerunner 15 counts your steps and the calories you burn all day. When you upload your data to Garmin Connect, this allows you to monitor your progress toward fitness goals and see overall fitness trends. Forerunner 15 also reminds you when it’s time to move if you’ve been sitting for more than an hour. When you get that cue to move, just walk for a couple of minutes to reset the inactivity indicator. Motivation by the Mile Forerunner 15 has an auto lap function that automatically alerts you when you complete each mile, giving you a friendly beep and a backlit screen that flashes your time for that mile. When you complete your run and save it, you’ll see a summary of how you did, including your total time, distance, average pace and calories. For

reviews.title: Watch is great, but software requires an afternoon of trial and error
reviews.text:
The concept behind the watch is excellent. I love it. If you're not a runner, you might find it unsatisfying however.

There aren't a lot of modes to learn. It hones in on tracking number of steps in a day. When you go for a run, the step tracker operates in the background while the stopwatch, heartrate monitor and calorie tracker take over. When you're done for your run, the step tracker becomes primary again, having added the steps from your run to the daily total.

But now we come to the software, which is unfortunately seemingly yet another case of software designers who don't know squat about the thing they're writing the software for. It's not the worst athletic performance user interface I ever used, but it's very ill conceived. While the watch is awesome for its focus and simplicity, the software is plagued with too many buttons, icons, panels, and drop down menus. It's a mess that a geek working for Garmin felt was best left for you to figure out rather than design it correctly. I'll probably find out it works just fine, but a user shouldn't have to put in multiple hours of time to do so.
reviews.author: AHKNC7ODVHOGTBQLHUYWL4SXZFQQ

reviews.title: No complaints: Updates
reviews.text:
I've had this for about a month, and so far so good. I use it for running on the weekends, and during the week I use it for circuit training in the gym. I used to use Polar, but after too many defective products, I switched.

I like the sturdy strap that comes with this. It's a solid plastic strap, but seems less bulky then my old one. The readout is easy to see. I keep it on BPM while exercising, but there are other settings as well. The GPS is usually working within a minute.

A few observations:
1. The calories burn estimate seems high (compared to the Polar). Averages about 200 calories higher on the Garmin per workout. Not sure which one is more accurate. Not a huge deal. I generally don't eat back exercise calories.

3. I wear a Fitbit One as well, and my Garmin usually counts at least 1,00 steps more than the Fitbit per day. I average at least 13,000 steps per day, so again, not a big deal to me. It may matter to someone else.

All in all, I'm very happy with my Garmin so far. Will update in another month.

Update: Still working fine. Absolutely no complaints.

Update: Died on Christmas Day. Didn't get wet, no warning, nothing. Just died. And to the list of HRM that don't work.

2nd update: I wiped the contacts on the back and it came back on. There was an update pending. Maybe the reason that it stopped?

Anyhow, it seems to be working fine again. We shall see

Update 7/7/2017 Still working fine. I've had no problems.
reviews.author: AGB4TQVVVLOHLQJ4MGFJU7EY2IFA

reviews.title: Garmin Website Woes May not be worth it.
reviews.text:
I have had my Garmin for a few months now. I cannot recommend this product to anyone at this time. There have been so many difficulties with the Garmin Connect website. I was patient and waited as they addressed the issues....two months later, there are still issues. I have lost data from my runs and other activities due to website "downage," inability to sync. and web syncing issues that delayed and then did not allow for uploading information due to the lack of prompt downloading.

Email and phone customer support is lacking a well. Everyone is polite, you just don't get correct information. I received an "I'm sorry your information didn't uploaded, but you can load the information manually." I thanked the man, hung up, then tried to manually put in my information only to be informed...."we're sorry, you cant manually input that information."

I wouldn't buy this until you know for certain that Garmin has improved its system.
reviews.author: AEMWZ6VFOCKC57RUC7CV2MEYFWEQ

reviews.title: I'd skip this
reviews.text:
I'm a casual runner who's done a couple half marathons, 10ks, 5ks. I really run to keep in shape and would run about 5 miles a day during the summer. First off, let me say that I bought this at the wrong time - I don't really run in the winter and I bought this right before the winter. That's my fault and wasn't the best time to buy this thing...

However, I have been able to get out there a few days and test this watch out. I live in a suburban neighborhood in NJ so there are a few trees but no tall buildings whatsoever. There are no cell / radio towers in my area as far as I know. But I'm no communications or IT expert, so I can't speak intelligently about radio interference.

Ok ok, on to the review of the damn thing. In a word, average. It takes at least 25 seconds to get the GPS signal on a good day and the last time I ran I was just standing there for 5 minutes before I said screw it and ran without the GPS. These signal-grabbing times simply don't cut it. And, by the way, these are all clear days. As far the GPS tracking, I've checked my route afterwards and it shows me about 10 feet off the path that I ran. Incidentally, it also mapped the same route with differences - some were as much as .09 miles. That might not seem like a whole lot, but it is.

All in all, I haven't gotten much utility out of this, notwithstanding that I bought it at the wrong time. I'll start running more in the coming months so I'll get more use out of it. I'd go with the FR10 if I could re-do.

As long as you can run with a phone (either in your hand or on your arm), that is a better choice. That way you don't have to download.  But this is a garmin and it's a top of the line watch. I find all of the GPS watches will also vary with distance and time anyway

reviews.title: Features failed one by one
reviews.text:
This watch/fitness tracker was very good for the first few days... then the "raise to wake" function started working sometimes, and sometimes not, and currently it does not function at all. Have tried toggling it many times via the app.....no help.
At first I was also impressed with the accuracy of the heart monitor and the Sp02.... especially the fact that the watch will continually monitor SP02.... something that I've not seen on any other Watch with SP02 capability, and my sole reason for owning any smart watch..... but again, in a few days the accuracy fell to 10-12 points off based on comparing with a finger oximeter that was calibrated at my Dr's office. It's as if the OS is slowly failing, feature by feature, with each charging of the watch.

I agree with another review that said its a "half baked" product. The OS is too limited/simple, and the required companion app needs serious work. Compared to comparable brands, it's ecosystem is non-existent. They advertise/boast of 40+ watch faces.... but they are all pretty sad, and the ability to create custom faces is so limited/bad, it might as well not even be on the watch.
This price range of smart watches has exploded recently, and gotten better and better. It's as if TicWatch either didn't know this fact, or completely ignored it. They are offering this watch that has fewer features, and a poorer OS, than many smart watches that are sold for nearly 1/2 the price. They either need to make a 2.0 version of this watch, and improve it greatly, or just pull it from the market.
reviews.author: AGWWLBKLGSMWQS44J7SHLDVFLLBQ

reviews.title: Great watch, much better than my old "smart" watch
reviews.text:
I got Moto 360 several years ago when smart watches first came out and it was nice, but annoying. It was tied into everything and kept giving me alerts and so eventually I got tired of it. My wife also had a first generation Samsung Gear watch and that was about the same as the Moto.
Well, fast forward a few years and now my wife, daughter, son and daughter-in-law all have newer smart watches (Apple Watches and Fitbit) and they all love them. It was time to get a new phone, so I got a Samsung S20 FE and then decided to get the Samsung watch. I saw on the reviews that it really works well with Samsung phones and so I decided to get the Watch 3. I was looking at the Watch 2, which had a few less features and then I found the Watch 3 in the 41mm size and then found a refurbished one so I ended up paying about the same for the Watch 3 as I would have for the Watch 2.
I'm very impressed with the watch and really love it. I didn't get the one with a cell phone, it just connects to my phone with bluetooth and/or wifi, but it's really amazing. I get my messages, and can turn off the ones that I don't want and then I also get all the health apps. So, now it tells me to get off my chair and walk around and does EKG and stress tesing and Oxygen levels..
I was concerned that the 41 mm might be too small, but it is a great size for me as you can see from the pic. I have had a Citizen Echo Drive for more than 10 years and it is a little larger, but I like the size of the Watch 3 better.
If you are thinking about getting a watch, this one is a good deal right now and I believe it does work best if you use it with a Samsung phone.

title: Garmin Forerunner 35 Watch, Black (Renewed)
description: The Forerunner 35 is Garmin's simple solution to gathering biometric training data during the simplest form of endurance training. It's built to be a stand-alone GPS running companion that handles everything from heart rate monitoring to route mapping with no phone or ancillary heart rate strap or footpod needed. Garmin assures us that you can "Just put on the Forerunner 35 and go"; however, since it's so unobtrusive and its benefits extend well beyond fitness training, chances are good you'll already have it on. Since that's the case, you can skip step one and proceed directly to "go." The Forerunner 35 dials down the functionality of some of Garmin's more expensive models, resulting in a simplified interface for tracking running and walking without the clutter of multisport features. It's something of a bridge between the vivofit and Forerunner lines, combining biometric and lifestyle data with the added oomph of GPS tracking and route mapping. Compared to the vivoactive, it sacrifices multisport functionality while adding the above-mentioned heart rate monitor. If you're primarily a pedestrian athlete, then the vivoactive's golf, swim, and cycling modes won't be missed. The heart rate monitor will prove invaluable, though, both during training sessions and your everyday life. While you're logging workouts, the Forerunner 35's GPS capabilities track distance and speed while mapping the route. While these features do extend to time in the saddle, the unit is designed primarily for running. The addition of Garmin Move IQ means the Forerunner 35 transitions seamlessly from running to walking while keeping a constant eye on steps taken. The optical, wrist-based heart rate monitor is a constant feature, though, and while it may not offer the precision of a trained medical professional constantly checking your pulse, it does keep you abreast of exertion levels including intensity minutes. This lets it manage interval training for you, letting you focus on exertion ... 2

reviews.title: Beats the Garmin Instinct Hands-Down In Usability
reviews.text:
I bought this watch renewed and paid about 1/3rd what I paid for my new Garmin Instinct last year. I will be selling the Garmin Instinct and keeping the Garmin Forerunner 35.

There are so many reviews, that I won't go into tremendous detail other than to say this watch is very efficient and intuitive, and you can't put a price on that. I simply walk out of the house, click two buttons, and start exercising. While I'm exercising, I don't have to deal with any nonsense. I can glance at the watch face if I want to know my heartrate or how long I've been walking/running, but I don't otherwise have to interact with the watch. Later, when I'm done with my walk or run, I can do a deep-dive with the Garmin app to see exactly where I went on a map (a huge feature for me, since I want to explore new places to walk/run), what parts of the walk/run were strenuous, at which points my heart rate or pace increased or decreased, etc.

My only complaint about this watch is all the notifications. I have to figure out how to turn them off, because they are useless. You see the first few words of a text or notification, but not who it's from, or not even which app sent the notification. I'm not being harsh: They really are useless notifications given the character limit on the watch face.

But this watch is a solid "buy." I love it otherwise.

reviews.title: Great fitness tracker
reviews.text:
I did a good amount of research on other devices before buying this product. I initially wanted a sleek smartwatch that also had great fitness tracking modes, but apparently you either get a pretty smartwatch/OK fitness tracker, or a decent fitness tracker/outdated looking smartwatch. In the end I decided I wanted mainly a fitness tracker that has some smartwatch features, and that I wouldn't use most of the features on an actual smartwatch.

PROS

The app is awesome! My husband is a software developer so I learn a lot about what makes good apps good and what makes bad apps bad. This app is free and user friendly. It has mostly accurate measurements and you can easily track your progress and areas of improvement. It also has a feature that allows whoever you send the link to to track your location, in case you are concerned about getting kidnapped on your run. Very good stuff.

The steps I get seem to be pretty consistent as well as the distance traveled. The heartrate tracker seems to be mostly accurate (checked with manual pulse check).

The battery life is pretty good. Obviously I would use more battery if I am using the fitness modes. Depending on your activity level and use of the fitness modes, the battery could last you all week plus some. The battery charges to full in about 30 minutes.

I like the rectangular watch face and the light green band. I also like that you can change the bands. Apparently for some of the expensive FitBit models the band is not changeable so some people just buy a whole new watch. Like whaaat?

CONS
I like being able to see the texts I get on the watch, but I don't really care for receiving phone calls (I get so many spam calls that I don't even answer my phone lol). I don't think there is a way to say 'no' to having phone calls, so I get frequent notifications to enable phone call permissions. It's not too much of a problem but it's kind of annoying sometimes.

I think the sleep tracker is interesting, but I don't think it is accurate. I think that as long as you are "resting" during the hours that you put as your sleeping hours under Settings, that it considers this as sleep. Sometimes I can't sleep but the device considers this as light sleep. I think this would be an interesting addition, that perhaps it could be integrated: I am a night shift worker and I switch between day/night sleep, so I have to manually enter my bedtimes in the app's User Settings to sleep. I think that if there was an activity button, like when you choose walk/run/cardio on the watch itself, for sleep, it would be easier for me personally. I am not sure if that could be done with a software update or if this is a hardware type thing.

FINAL THOUGHTS

I wear this watch almost 24/7 and I think it has helped me consider and evaluate my activity levels as well as track my improvement. I think that if Garmin made a watch that had the aesthetics and watch interface comparable to the Apple watch but kept the good fitness aspect, I would definitely purchase as an upgrade.

reviews.title: Poor Heart Rate tracking - only 2 Green LEDs
reviews.text:
I’m a former Fitbit Blaze, Fitbit Ionic and Withings Steel user. As another reviewer mentioned, two basic features of any activity tracker is step counting and heart rate tracking. The luxe fails on the latter. The luxe dropped the 3rd green LED and replaced it with an SpO2 sensor. While tracking oxygen levels during sleep is an interesting idea and could potentially provide valuable insights, the reliability hr tracking hit of losing 33% of the HR sensors is huge. Fitbit did something similar going from the Blaze to the Ionic. My Ionics HR tracking with 2 green LEDs was horrible as well. The most reliable HR tracker is the Apple Watch which uses a combination of green LEDs and infrared LEDs. Anyway, I’m convinced that the 3 LEDs of the Vivomove HR are superior to the Luxe.

As others have mentioned the screen is difficult to see in even moderate sunlight. I found the touchscreen difficult to use and I couldn’t get the tilt to turn on to work reliable. The watch band was a bit too small even on my rather small male left wrist. Regarding the readability in sunlight, I would exchange battery life for a brighter screen.

I’m not sure why Garmin is having issues with IOS 13 but I would periodically have to toggle my Bluetooth off/on to get the luxe recognized in the Connect app. I’m sure they’ll resolve this issue but it is annoying for a $500 device.

I’ve decided to give the Premium Vivomove HR a chance. It’s $200 cheaper and has the 3 Green LEDs for HR tracking. I just received the device this afternoon. For some reason, even though the watch looks virtually indistinguishable from the Luxe, it seems to be a little more comfortable sitting on my wrist. Right away, I noticed that the tilt to view works well and the touchscreen seems more responsive. It’s too early to report on the HR tracking.

reviews.title: Good looking, pricey watch, but inaccurate measurements (compared to my multiple Fitbits)
reviews.text:
I have had this Garmin for 6 weeks and I am disappointed I got it. I have had 5 different Fitbit models over many years, but when I switched to the latest Mac OS (Catalina), it was not compatible and the Fitbit wouldn't SYNC.
So I looked into the Garmin models. I get a discount on my healthcare plan if I take part in a "Million Steps Challenge". Certain fitness trackers have a compatible app and share your results.
I am disappointed in the unreliable battery life of this Garmin. Sometimes the battery dies after 2 ½ days, other times after 4 days. This model features sweep hands to look more like a watch, than a fitness tracker. They magically move out of the way, so you can see your steps, or, heart rate, or the weather etc. Very cool, but sometimes they freeze up and won't change modes, until I plug it in to my power source and it seems to reboot itself.
Another issue, it is supposed to count the number of flights of stairs I do in a day. I often get credit for only 3 flights a day, when I do way more than that. One day it recorded that I had descended stairs 5 times, but only ascended once?? I had counted at least 8 times, both up and down.
The sleep tracker has been recording that I have been getting a solid, undisturbed 8 + hrs of sleep most nights. This is sooooo not true. I am lucky to get 4 hours of sleep many nights. I am always up at least once if not several times, my hubbie and I both retired a few months back, and we are in a pandemic, and we are trying to move back to Canada from Alaska, while the Canadian border remains closed. Lots of stress (which the Garmin was also supposed to record, but it doesn't). My daughter's Apple watch, tells her to "Breathe" when it detects a rise in stress level (she's a teacher).
So, overall, I do not recommend this Garmin Vivomove Smartwatch.
reviews.author: AFTMDRPFZWU3WQ4N7AUVLCDA7SVA

reviews.title: Renewed Galaxy Smartwatch
reviews.text:
Refurbished Aspect of this Review:
This isn't my first time ordering a refurbished product from Amazon. I expected the watch to come with the original packaging, unsealed and possibly missing some components inside like inserts and whatnot... Everytime I've ordered Renewed it came in the original packaging... I could get over not having the box fine, (unless I was giving it as a gift.) but what really disappointed me was it didn't include the manual.

It's also important to note that you cannot find the manual in PDF on the Samsung website. They have the manuals for all their other watches, but not this one for some reason. So just keep that in mind.
I still don't know the full functionality of this watch and getting to know it has been a bit frustrating without instructions. You can find review YouTube videos that have some instructions, but you will not be shown everything...

With that out the way, the watch itself is in pristine condition. No scratches or anything. You wouldn't be able to tell it's used. The charger itself did have some wear. The lettering on the back of it is rubbed off in the middle. Not a big deal.

Functionality and Quality Part of this Review:
You know, I bought a cheap end smart watch before getting this one. I didn't get the cheapest one. It was midrange in pricing... I really thought paying so much for a brand name watch is a ripoff before I purchased one. They must work the same when they have similar specs right? NO.
The knockoff watch gave me bizarre heart rate readings. Said I took steps in my sleep, and don't even get me started on the sleep monitor. Be asleep for thirty minutes, wide awake for ten, straight to REM sleep after that for two minutes... It's a joke. The only positive thing I could say about the knockoff is it had 50% better battery life. But again, it was so useless that I wish it would run out of battery.

The body readings on this Galaxy watch are SO accurate that it's a little creepy. Plus it has features that the others don't... Like stress level! Bruh.
This was well worth the money I am very happy with how accurate it is!

One thing that surprises me, and is a bit disappointing is you cannot use the watch to answer calls when you are strictly using Bluetooth and wifi. You need to use a cell service which means you need to pay your mobile carrier more monthly to use the calling feature. With the knockoff you can just use Bluetooth. It sets your watch like a speaker or headphones so you can answer the call...
Same for texting. When I get a text it notifies me but to answer it I have to use my phone.
When someone calls me my watch doesn't ring or tell me that someone is calling. I only get a notification when I miss the call.

It's possible I am just doing something wrong as I don't have the manual... But just some stuff I wanted to share that I would've found helpful!
reviews.author: AHKLYD5GR4BP4K65HR6EGXRNP47A

Equipped with GPS + GLONASS for precise tracking of your daily steps, distance

reviews.title: Probably the best bang for the buck in this brand, but needs lots of improvements
reviews.text:
I've owned/worn this watch for about 2 weeks now, and having had other Amazfit watches, this is probably the best bang for the buck in this brand. That being said, there is a LOT of room for improvement in this watch.

-It's sleep tracking has proven to be only marginally accurate. It has been off as much as 2 hours either way compared to reality.
-SP02 reading cannot be set for auto. It must be done manually every time. It is very finicky and it usually take multiple attempts without it failing to read. When it does give a reading, it is very accurate, but is annoying to get there.
-"Raise to Wake" function is very "hit and miss"...... unless your standing and make the full motion with your arm, it doesn't "wake". I am yet to have it work when I am in a sitting position.
-The Walking "exercise mode" is woefully inaccurate. (I've not tested any of the others) I have a common route that I walk daily, which is 1.2 miles. The watch varies every time on the distance, reading from .5 to .87 miles. This tells me the GPS function has issues.
-DO NOT believe the claimed battery life on this watch!! Unless you turn off EVERYTHING that you can, the best you will get for battery life is 3 full and part of a 4th days/nights on a charge.
-Charging this watch is really odd. If charged from near zero, it gets to fully charge much faster then if it's charged above 50%. From near zero it will charge in the time the company states. But at 50% or more, charging to 100% takes 3+ hours!
-"Auto Brightness" needs work. When set to auto, the brightness levels are far too low for easy viewing in any light conditions... especially outdoors. I've had to disable the auto brightness and just accept the added battery drain.
Now the Good things....
-The build quality is good.
-Comfortable to wear
-Heart monitor is accurate and is automatic.
- SP02 is accurate, although the fact that it only works manually, and usually takes several tries to get a reading is annoying.

Conclusion: For the price point, this watch is just OK. Obviously there are many more cons than good things in my review.
I am simply not one who accepts anything less than the advertised claims for any product. All too often people take the attitude... "What do you expect for the price?" Those who take that attitude are the ones directly responsible for the way things are today.... too lazy to lift a finger and demand that a product meets the advertising claims, so companies get away with false advertising, flat out lies, and it just keeps getting worse and worse.
reviews.author: AGWWLBKLGSMWQS44J7SHLDVFLLBQ

reviews.title: Most under-rated brand
reviews.text:
I'm a big fan of the Mi Band series of trackers, and this is the first device that finally got me to switch to something else. If you aren't familiar, Xiaomi and Huami aren't big brands in the US compared to other countries, but these are solid high quality products and not your standard fare Chinese junk. I've tried a LOT of different fitness trackers and smart watches- Everything from Google WearOS, Fitbit, Apple, and all the way down to the cheap trackers from Chinese throwaway brands with obscure apps. For a long time I've said I liked the features and quality of the expensive brands, but found they all had major flaws (battery life being a major one- I don't want another thing to charge as often as a phone!). The Mi band from Xiaomi was super affordable and high quality compared to others in that price range, and had a battery that only required being charged once a month.
This is basically the smarter older brother of that band. It is compatible with Mi Fit, so all my data transferred right over, but it is so much nicer than the band. For starters, the notification system is easier to read and keep up with messages, but the benefits go far beyond a larger screen. The music control is a nice touch, I like being able to pause and adjust the volume without taking my phone out of my pocket if playing on a BT speaker while I'm doing dishes, for example. The tracking is pretty accurate compared to other expensive brands, and while it doesn't support apps like Wear OS and Apple Watches, it has all the fitness functions you'd need built in already, including some fun widgets beyond the expected ones like a stopwatch, phone finder and even a compass! And unlike cheap brands, the UI is responsive and smooth. Not the smoothest out there for sure, but more than acceptably smooth considering how much a flagship smartwatch costs compared to this.
It has support for voice commands, but I honestly found that feature kind of gimmicky more than useful. The higher end version of this includes a speaker which might make the command function more useful as sort of a digital assistant, not to mention the ability to take phone calls from your wrist. But the model I got to try out lacks the speaker, which is a fine compromise for a cheaper price tag all things considered. The battery life at first was just OK, a few days on a charge if using lift-to-wake functions, but just as on my Mi Band tracker of old, I found if I turn off auto-wake and rely on using the button to wake the display, the battery life goes from counting in days to counting in weeks. That's the killer feature of this device- the ability to leave it on to track fitness and sleep habits without having to charge it so often. I'll have to charge it more than my old Mi Band's monthly top up for sure, probably at least 2x a month, but that's still very manageable.
So that's it, after years of loving my Mi Bands and considering it the standard by which all practical fitness trackers should be judged, I'm finally moving on to an Amazfit watch. This is a difficult value to beat.

reviews.title: Great battery life but that's it
reviews.text: I was so excited about this watch because of the great battery life and it really was great. I used every setting I could ever need like always on display, tilt to wake, full brightness etc and I still easily got 7 days on a single charge which is pretty much unheard of for smart watches. The issue that I had is with the software. The app notifications were not great and there were very few options on customizing them. For instance every time I played a new song on oandora it vibrated and flash the song name which became pretty annoying. I also couldn't get Alexa to work no matter how hard I tried and I even reached out to customer support and they never answered me. I wish this ran wear os or another software that was better but the hardware is great. But sadly not great enough to make this the watch I'll wear every day.
reviews.author: AHDXQCPXT6VYDEEZORX32ZIS5ZHQ

reviews.title: So far so good
reviews.text:
Stainless steel version has a little weight to it. The display is big if you prefer a bigger display. Always on heart rate and stress monitor are a nice touch. I'd love to see an option in the future to enable always on SPO2 monitoring. Body temperature cannot be recorded on the GTR 2 model. I'd love to see that added in the future. Battery life is good so far. Any smartwatch that you don't have to charge every day or throughout the day is a plus. You can change the strap with any quick release 22mm strap. That way, you can use the silicone band for sport and switch it up to a leather band or even a link band to dress it up when you're out on the town.

The price is phenomenal for what you get compared to other smart watches. It can be found on sale here on Amazon from time to time. It works with both iOS and Android. I recommend giving it a try. There's an Amazfit style to fit everyone's needs and style.
